items.title: New US import tariffs pressuring car exports | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US President Donald Trump’s tariff package is a blow to the global economy. Cars from the EU are subject to 25% import duties. At Europe’s largest vehicle loading terminal in Bremerhaven, Germany, there are fears of major consequences.

items.title: How willing is the US to still support Ukraine? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>European countries have pledged an additional 21-Bbillion euros of military aid for Ukraine. The promises came at a meeting of the Ukraine Defense Contact Group in Brussels. The US has played a leading role in the forum since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine. But that's been scaled back since President Donald Trump moved into the White House. US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth made a last-minute appearance by video link. 

Chapters:
00:00 Ukraine allies pledge over €20bn in aid
03:05 Joshua Shifrinson, associate professor with the School of Public Policy at Maryland University

items.title: Is a two-state solution still possible? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu says Israel's renewed military pressure in Gaza is aimed at forcing Hamas to agree to a new deal and release hostages. But many Palestinians fear the buffer zone is the first step toward an Israeli occupation.
The UN has issued a stark warning, saying Israel's actions risk destroying "the future viability of the Palestinians as a group" in Gaza.

items.title: Why has the US' Greenland military base commander been fired? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The head of the US military base in Greenland has been fired after criticising the Trump administration's plans for the Arctic island. Colonel Susannah Meyers - seen here on the left - met with Vice President JD Vance during his visit to Greenland two weeks ago. While he was there, the Vice President criticised Denmark's control of Greenland - a resource-rich island which President Trump wants the US to take over. Colonel Meyers is reported to have distanced herself from Mr Vance's comments, saying they were not reflective of the military base. 
More information is provided by William Glucroft, US analyst for The Parliament Magazine.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Three theories of Trump’s strategy for American power | DW Analysis
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Does Donald Trump have a foreign policy strategy? Many assume he is too disruptive to follow one. But it might not be so simple. Plenty of his actions do align with established strategic thinking – which could help us anticipate what comes next. In this video, DW’s Richard Walker unpacks three theories: 
1) That Trump is a foreign policy ‘Realist’ 
2) That he plans to attempt a diplomatic coup known as a ‘Reverse Kissinger’
3) That he is pursuing a vision of geopolitical ‘Spheres of Influence’ 
Historian of US foreign policy Stephen Wertheim spells out each strategy, the goals and the risks versus rewards. And analysts Gautam Chikermane, Liana Fix, Alexander Gabuev, and Amanda Hsiao examine the consequences for the rest of the world, with a special focus on Europe, India, Russia, Ukraine, China, and Taiwan. 



Chapters:

0:00 Intro
3:22 Realism
5:50 International order
7:23 America First
9:34 “Realist businessman”
11:05 Realism and NATO
12:01 Europe responds
14:46 Reverse Kissinger
17:10 Variants on Kissinger
19:05 Russians are ready
20:14 US calculations
21:25 Russian calculations
22:34 Playing Trump?
25:29 Nuclear deal?
26:28 China’s calculations
27:42 Impact
30:43 Spheres of influence
31:50 Greenland
33:41 Historical echoes
36:09 Imperial expansionism?
39:44 Deal of the century?
42:36 “Very dangerous world”
44:35 Taiwan
46:39 Theaters interlinked
48:08 Unintended consequences


#DonaldTrump #USforeignpolicy #trumppolicies

items.title: How difficult would it be to hold legitimate elections in Ukraine? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Ukraine’s willingness to negotiate a ceasefire with Russia has revived an old question - when will the country be able to hold elections? Ukraine has been under martial law, which prohibits elections, since February 2022, when Russia launched its full-scale invasion. Reports suggest President Zelenskyy has ordered his team to organize a vote if a truce takes effect soon. IF that happened, a general election could take place as early as this summer - at a time when President Zelenskyy’s trust ratings have spiked. 

Chapters:
00:00 Is Ukraine mulling elections in summer 2025?
04:06 Orysia Lutsevych, Head of Ukraine Forum of Chatham House
09:11 Olena Davlikanova, Friedrich Ebert Foundation

items.title: Dizzying escalation of US-China tariffs threatens global economy | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The escalation of tariffs has unraveled a trade relationship between the United States and China forged over decades. Beijing has increased tariffs on American imports from 84 to 125 percent starting on Saturday. The move escalates the ongoing trade war between the world's two biggest economies and is in response to the US raising taxes on Chinese goods to 145 percent. The tarifs are jeopardizing the fate of two superpowers and threatening to drag down the world economy. 

Chapters:
00:00 China raises tariffs on US goods to 125%
03:00 Fraser Howie, Economist and Author
07:26 Steven Beardsley, DW Business

items.title: Trump envoy Witkoff meets Russian counterpart in Moscow | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US envoy Steve Witkoff is in Russia, as Washington continues its attempts to broker a ceasefire in Ukraine. It's Mr Witkoff's third official visit to the country. The Kremlin declined to say whether Mr Witkoff would meet Russian President Vladimir Putin - but said an announcement will be made if such a meeting happens. The pair met during the envoy's previous trips to Russia.

items.title: China raises US tariffs from 84% to 125% | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>China has just raised its tariffs against the US from 84 percent to 125 percent starting Saturday. Beijing says it will ignore any further tariff hikes from Washington. The news sent European stocks plummeting. These are live pictures from the Frankfurt Stock Exchange of Germany's DAX  stock index. The day had started optimistically for investors before Beijing's announcement. The United Nations trade agency has said the tariffs will have a catastrophic impact on developing nations. China says the US should bear full responsibility for the global economic turbulence.

#Tariffs #TradeWar #ChinaUS

items.title: Will Merz's coalition deal do the economic trick? | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germany's likely next government has published its much-awaited coalition agreement. The political marriage document between Friedrich Merz's conservative CDU/CSU party and the Social Democrat SPD marks a clamp down on migration. It also promises a huge boost for Germany's struggling economy. But will Merz's plan to stop the deindustrialization of Germany work? In this episode of Berlin Briefing, DW's Chief Political Editor Michaela Küfner asks economist Peter Bofinger how far this roadmap for economic recovery can take Germany and Europe. DW's Chief Political Correspondent Nina Haase and Business Reporter Steven Beardsley join the debate on whether the incoming government has a plan that works on the economy, defence and beyond.

CHAPTERS:
00:00 Intro
01:33 How did the markets react?
05:20 Merz's message to Trump
08:39 Lars Klingbeil – remember the name
14:50 Faith in Germany again?
16:16 How did the AfD react?
19:22 Ducking the demographics
22:05 What does the 'Mittelstand' want?
24:44 Migration - what does the agreement say?
31:25 Defense spending boost
42:00 Can they make it?

items.title: US stocks resume free fall as Trump teases China deal | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The world's two largest economies appear locked in a tit-for-tat tariff escalation as Beijing announces a massive new levy on US imports. Markets continue to be rattled amid the global economic uncertainty. China said it would impose a 125% tariff on US goods starting Saturday, up from the previous 84%, the Finance Ministry announced on Friday. "The US's imposition of abnormally high tariffs on China seriously violates international trade rules, basic economic laws and common sense," Beijing's State Council Tariff Commission said in a Finance Ministry statement, adding that the new levy comes into effect on Saturday. The move follows US President Donald Trump's decision to raise duties on Chinese goods to 145%, ratcheting up a trade war that threatens to upend global supply chains. The White House has kept the pressure on the world's second-largest economy and second-biggest provider of US imports by singling it out for an additional tariff increase, having suspended most of the so-called "reciprocal" duties imposed on dozens of other countries. The US  duties on Chinese products have "become a numbers game with no practical significance in economics", a spokesperson for Beijing's commerce ministry said. "It will only further expose the United States's... bullying and coercion. It will become a joke."

00:00 US China tariff dispute: Latest developments
02:10 How does China react to Trump's latest moves
05:15 Europe pauses retaliatory tariffs


For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/
